随着人们认识到这种耕作模式是气候友好、环境可持续和有利可图的，对农林复合系统的投资也在增加。
最近，创业公司Propagate Ventures筹集了150万美元的种子资金，帮助美国八个州的农民从传统农业过渡到农林复合农业。
从个人农场到咖啡公司，以及世界上一些最大的巧克力公司目前都在投资农林复合经营。
相对较长的等待时间，直到盈利能力使投资者和农民的采用变得复杂，这通常需要快速回报和对股东的义务，这突显了像Propagate这样的公司的利基市场。
2016年下半年，伊桑·斯坦伯格(Ethan Steinberg)和他的两个朋友计划驾车穿越美国采访农民。他们的目标是解开一个困扰他们每个人一段时间的谜语。他们想知道，为什么美国农业基本上忽略了树木？
这不是什么深奥的调查。根据越来越多的科学研究，将树木纳入农田有益于从土壤健康到作物生产再到气候的方方面面。斯坦伯格和他的朋友杰里米·考夫曼(Jeremy Kaufman)和哈里森·格林(Harrison Greene)也怀疑这可能会产生其他东西：钱。
斯坦伯格回忆道：“我们注意到有很多关于整体放牧、免耕、覆盖种植的讨论和资本转移。”他指的是最近引起环境和商业关注的一些土地和气候友好型农业做法。“我们想，那树木呢？就在那时，一个灯泡熄灭了。“。
这三人创建了Propagate Ventures，这是一家现在为农民提供基于软件的经济分析、实地项目管理和投资者融资的公司，帮助将树木和树木作物添加到农业模型中。斯坦伯格解释说，Propagate的主要目标之一是将资金从感兴趣的投资者手中转移到需要资金的农民手中-他认为这是这种以树木为基础的农业的长期障碍。
宣传很快就引起了人们的注意。在过去的两年里，总部设在纽约和科罗拉多州的该组织已经扩展到八个州，主要是在东北部和大西洋中部，现在正在与20个不同的农场合作。上个月，它宣布从波士顿的被忽视的气候机会获得了150万美元的种子资金，该机会是杰里米和汉内洛尔·格兰瑟姆环境信托基金(Jeremy And Hannelore Grantham Environmental Trust)的全资子公司。
该信托基金的投资官埃里克·史密斯(Eric Smith)表示：“我希望他们能帮助农民实现生产系统的多样化，并封存碳。”“在一个完美的世界里，我们应该有美国10%到20%的土地生产用于农林复合经营。”
在过去的几年里，私营部门对“可持续”和“气候友好”努力的兴趣飙升。美国银行主题投资主管哈伊姆·伊斯雷尔在今年早些时候的世界经济论坛上表示，到2025年，气候解决方案市场可能会从今天的1万亿美元翻一番，达到2万亿美元。根据金融服务公司晨星(Morningstar)的数据，流入美国可持续基金的资金一直在戏剧性地增加，即使在新冠肺炎疫情期间也创下了纪录。
虽然农业投资只是这些数字中的一小部分，但有迹象表明，对“再生农业”的投资也在迅速增加。“再生农业”是一种比地球更好的做法，而不是退化的做法。在2019年的一份报告中，总部位于北卡罗来纳州达勒姆的研究机构克罗坦研究所(Croatan Institute)发现，按照再生农业标准，美国有价值约475亿美元的投资资产。
克罗坦研究所(Croatan Institute)高级研究员大卫·勒扎克斯(David LeZaks)表示：“美国和全球的资本格局确实在发生变化。”“人们开始问更多的问题，他们的钱是如何为他们工作的，因为它与经济回报有关，或者在创造采掘型经济、气候变化或劳工问题上，它可能会对他们不利。”
农林业，古老的将树木结合到农业中的做法，只是再生农业的一个子集，而再生农业本身就是更大的“ESG”或环境、社会和治理投资世界的子集。但根据史密斯和斯坦伯格的说法，加上数量不多但数量不断增加的金融家、企业家和公司高管，它是一个投资特别成熟的公司。
尽管农林复合经营在美国相对罕见，但在全球范围内却是一种广泛的农业实践。对气候变化解决方案进行排名的气候变化缓解智库Project Dumdown估计，目前约有6.5亿公顷(16亿英亩)的土地处于农林复合系统中；其他组织认为这个数字更高。根据支持者的说法，这些系统的回报估计也很可观。
Ernst Götsch是再生农业领域的领导者，他估计农林复合系统可以创造比传统农业多8倍的利润。德国森林金融公司(Forest Finance)的创始人哈里·阿森马赫(Harry Assenmacher)在2019年的一次采访中表示，他预计投资回报率至少在4%至7%之间；他的公司已经向投资者支付了750万美元的收益，预计稍后还会产生更多收入。森林金融公司将投资者与可持续林业和农林项目联系起来。
这导致人们对农林复合经营产生了广泛的营利性兴趣。在马萨诸塞州西部的一片狭长土地上，有像Propagate这样的小型初创公司，也有像马丁·安德顿(Martin Anderton)和乔诺·尼格尔(Jono Neiger)这样的小农民，他们在新的栗树旁边养鸡。在墨西哥，有机消费者协会的联合创始人兼国际主任罗尼·卡明斯(Ronnie Cummins)正在向投资者募集资金，以支持一个新的龙舌兰农林项目。像Dean‘s Beans这样的小型咖啡公司正在使用这种耕作方法，美国前副总统阿尔·戈尔(Al Gore)的卡尼福克农场(Caney Fork Farm)等较大的农场也在使用这种耕作方法。世界上一些最大的巧克力公司正在投资农林复合经营。
“我们确实看到私营部门的兴趣与日俱增，”迪特马尔·斯托安(Dietmar Stoian)表示。他是研究集团世界农林(World AgroForest)价值链、私营部门参与和投资的首席科学家。世界农林也是ICRAF的首字母缩写。“对他们中的一些人来说，农林复合经营的想法是相当新的。”
他和其他人说，部分原因是人们对农林间作的气候益处的认识不断增强。
根据“缩水工程”的说法，农林间作是从空气中吸收碳的最好的自然方法之一。该组织将森林畜牧业(一种将树木和牲畜结合在一起的方法)列为世界上第九大最具影响力的气候变化解决方案，超过了屋顶太阳能、电动汽车和地热能。
如果农民将林木种植面积从现在的约5.5亿公顷增加到2050年的约7.7亿公顷(13.6亿英亩到19亿英亩)，减少的二氧化碳排放量估计可以在这30年内减少高达420亿吨-根据NOAA的数据，这足以抵消2015年全球人类排放的所有二氧化碳-并可能带来2060亿至2730亿美元的投资回报。
农林业做法如此气候友好的部分原因(没有牲畜的系统，即“正常”农林系统，如遮荫种植的咖啡，据估计也可以通过减少量获得良好的投资回报，同时每年每公顷每年封存4.45吨碳)是因为它们所替代的东西。
例如，传统的畜牧业是碳密集型的。树木被砍伐成牧场，化石燃料被用作饲料的肥料，这些饲料被跨境运输，有时甚至是全球运输，使用更多的化石燃料。
在集中动物饲养作业(CAFO)中饲养的牲畜比吃草的奶牛产生更多的甲烷。另一方面，森林牧场系统包括在牧场种植树木-或者至少不砍伐它们。农民将牲畜从一个地方轮换到另一个地方，使土壤能够容纳更多的碳。
其他类型的农林复合实践也有类似的好处。例如，森林农业涉及在森林树冠下种植各种作物-这一过程可以改善生物多样性和土壤质量，还可以支持农场的根系和固碳潜力。
活动组织Might Earth的高级活动总监埃特尔·希贡内特(Etelle Higonnet)表示，越来越多的巧克力公司表示有兴趣纳入农林复合实践-与她第一次开始倡导这种方法时相比，这是一个明显的转变。
“当我们第一次与巧克力公司和贸易商谈论农林业时，几乎每个人都认为我是个疯子，”她说。“但三年过去了，几乎每一家主要的巧克力公司和可可贸易商都在制定农林复合计划。”
不过，她说，这在当地意味着什么，可能会有很大的不同。大多数情况下，推动农林复合投资的是公司的可持续发展部门，而不是高管。一些公司承诺100%从农林复合系统采购可可。其他人则满足于5%的可可来自使用农林业的农场。
她指出，一家公司所认为的“农林”也可能是模糊的--这种情况让她和其他气候倡导者担心公司会用这个词来“绿化”，或者基本上假装是环保的，而没有做出实质性的改变。
“什么是农林业？”北美气候焦点组织的执行董事西蒙·科尼格(Simon Konig)说。“没有明确的定义。有一个学术的、哲学的定义，但没有一个实际的定义，没有说“它包括这么多种类”。基本上，农林业是你想要的任何东西，也是你想要写在宣传册上的任何东西。“。
他说，他在南美看到过一些案例，人们努力将退化的养牛场改造成可可种植园。他们在可可旁边种植了香蕉树，可可在幼时需要遮荫。但当可可种了五年，需要更多的阳光时，农民就会把香蕉拿出来。
“他们说，‘这是农林业，’”科尼格说。“因此存在误解--有不同的目标和标准。”
他一直致力于为可可和巧克力公司制作一本实用的农林指南。他说，该指南的主要收获之一是，对于农林复合经营没有一刀切的方法。这取决于气候、目标、市场和各种其他变量。
克罗坦研究所的勒扎克斯说，这是农林复合经营迟迟没有引起投资者关注的原因之一。
“目前确实没有技术资源--基础设施、产品--来支持农林部门，”LeZaks说。
虽然农林业被视为碳抵消市场的巨大潜力，但其多变性使其成为一项更复杂的农业投资。农林复合投资的另一个挑战是时间。
树木作物需要数年时间才能生产坚果、浆果或木材。这对农民来说可能是一个障碍，他们往往多年没有多余的资金来捆绑。
“人们受困于一切照旧的生意，”来自世界农林公司的斯托安说。“他们必须向股东汇报。定期报告。这几乎与农林复合经营的长期性相矛盾。“。
这就是斯坦伯格和Propagate Ventures的用武之地。斯坦伯格说，该公司工作的第一部分是全面分析农民的经营情况。它评估业务目标，使用地理信息系统(GIS)组件来绘制土地图，并确定最适合特定农业系统的树木。通过软件分析，Propagate预测了长期成本收入比和产量，这是农民和可能的私人投资者的关键信息。
在分析阶段之后，PROPACTATE帮助实施农林复合系统。它还致力于将第三方投资者与农民联系起来，使用收入分享模式，即投资者从收获的树木作物和木材中提取一定比例的利润。
此外，Propagate致力于安排与有意将农林产品添加到其供应链中的买家签订商业合同。
斯坦伯格说：“这是一个与农民合作的机会，通过以一种特定于背景的方式将树木作物纳入他们的经营中，以提高盈利能力。”“它还开始解决我们在农业和其他领域面临的生态挑战。”
本报告是蒙古包持续报道全球农林复合趋势的一部分，请单击此处查看完整系列。
斯蒂芬妮·哈尼斯(Stephanie Hanes)是一名记者，著有“白人的游戏：拯救动物，重建伊甸园和其他非洲保护神话”(The White Man‘s Game：Save Animals，Rebuild Eden and Other。她在耶鲁大学环境学院任教。
note_en:
Investments in agroforestry systems are growing along with the recognition that this model of farming is climate-friendly, environmentally sustainable, and profitable.
 Recently the startup company Propagate Ventures raised $1.5 million in seed funding to help farmers in eight U.S. states transition from conventional agriculture to agroforestry.
 For-profits ranging from individual farms to coffee companies and some of the largest chocolate companies in the world are currently investing in agroforestry.
 The relatively longer wait until profitability complicates its adoption by investors and farmers alike, where quick returns and obligations to shareholders are normally required, underscoring the niche for companies like Propagate.
  In the latter part of 2016, Ethan Steinberg and two of his friends planned a driving tour across the U.S. to interview farmers. Their goal was to solve a riddle that had been bothering each of them for some time. Why was it, they wondered, that American agriculture basically ignored trees?
 This was no esoteric inquiry. According to a growing body of scientific research, incorporating trees into farmland benefits everything from soil health to crop production to the climate. Steinberg and his friends, Jeremy Kaufman and Harrison Greene, also suspected it might yield something else: money.
 “We had noticed there was a lot of discussion and movement of capital into holistic grazing, no till, cover cropping,” Steinberg recalls, referencing some of the land- and climate-friendly agricultural practices that have been garnering environmental and business attention recently. “We thought, what about trees? That’s when a lightbulb went off.”
 The trio created  Propagate Ventures, a company that now offers farmers software-based economic analysis, on-the-ground project management, and investor financing to help add trees and tree crops to agricultural models. One of Propagate’s key goals, Steinberg explained, was to get capital from interested investors to the farmers who need it — something he saw as a longtime barrier to this sort of tree-based agriculture.
 Propagate quickly started attracting attention. Over the past two years, the group, based in New York and Colorado has expanded into eight states, primarily in the Northeast and Mid-Atlantic, and is now working with 20 different farms. Last month, it announced that it had received $1.5 million in seed funding from Boston-based Neglected Climate Opportunities, a wholly owned subsidiary of the Jeremy and Hannelore Grantham Environmental Trust.
  “My hope is that they can help farmers diversify their production systems and sequester carbon,” says Eric Smith, investment officer for the trust. “In a perfect world, we’d have 10 to 20 percent of U.S. land production in agroforestry.”
 For the past few years, private sector interest in “sustainable” and “climate-friendly” efforts has skyrocketed. Haim Israel, Bank of America’s head of thematic investment, suggested at the World Economic Forum earlier this year that the climate solutions market could double from $1 trillion today to $2 trillion by 2025. Flows to sustainable funds in the U.S. have been increasing dramatically, setting records even amid the COVID-19 pandemic, according to the financial services firm Morningstar.
 And while agriculture investment is only a small subset of these numbers, there are signs that investments in “regenerative agriculture,” practices that improve rather degrade than the earth, are also increasing rapidly. In a 2019  report, the Croatan Institute, a research institute based in Durham, North Carolina, found some $47.5 billion worth of investment assets in the U.S. with regenerative agriculture criteria.
 “The capital landscape in the U.S. and globally is really shifting,” says David LeZaks, senior fellow at the Croatan Institute. “People are beginning to ask more questions about how their money is working for them as it relates to financial returns, or how it might be working against them in the creation of extractive economies, climate change or labor issues.”
 Agroforestry, the ancient practice of incorporating trees into farming, is just one subset of regenerative agriculture, which itself is a subset of the much larger “ESG,” or Environmental, Social and Governance, investment world. But according to Smith and Steinberg, along with a small but growing number of financiers, entrepreneurs and company executives, it is one particularly ripe for investment.
 Although relatively rare in the U.S., agroforestry is a widespread agricultural practice across the globe. Project Drawdown, a climate change mitigation think tank that ranks climate solutions, estimates that some 650 million hectares (1.6 billion acres) of land are currently in agroforestry systems; other groups put the number even higher. And the estimates for returns on those systems are also significant, according to proponents.
  Ernst Götsch, a leader in the regenerative agriculture world, estimates that agroforestry systems can create  eight times more profit than conventional agriculture. Harry Assenmacher, founder of the German company Forest Finance, which connects investors to sustainable forestry and agroforestry projects, said  in a 2019 interview that he expects between 4% and 7% return on investments at least; his company had already paid out $7.5 million in gains to investors, with more income expected to be generated later.
 This has led to a wide variety of for-profit interest in agroforestry. There are small startups, such as Propagate, and small farmers, such as Martin Anderton and Jono Neiger,  who raise chickens alongside new chestnut trees on a swath of land in western Massachusetts. In Mexico, Ronnie Cummins, co-founder and international director of the Organic Consumers Association, is courting investors for funds to support a new agave agroforestry project. Small coffee companies, such as  Dean’s Beans, are using the farming method, as are larger farms, such as former U.S. vice president Al Gore’s Caney Fork Farms. Some of the largest chocolate companies in the world are investing in agroforestry.
 “We are indeed seeing a growing interest from the private sector,” says Dietmar Stoian, lead scientist for value chains, private sector engagement and investments with the research group World Agroforestry, also known by the acronym ICRAF. “And for some of them, the idea of agroforestry is quite new.”
 Part of this, he and others say, is growing awareness about agroforestry’s climate benefits.
  According to Project Drawdown, agroforestry practices are some of the best natural methods to pull carbon out of the air. The group  ranked silvopasture, a method that incorporates trees and livestock together, as the ninth most impactful climate change solution in the world, above rooftop solar power, electric vehicles and geothermal energy.
 If farmers increased silvopasture acreage from approximately 550 million hectares today to about 770 million hectares by 2050 (1.36 billion acres to 1.9 billion acres), Drawdown estimated carbon dioxide emissions could be reduced over those 30 years by up to 42 gigatons — more than enough to offset all of the carbon dioxide emitted by humans globally in 2015,  according to NOAA — and could return $206 billion to $273 billion on investment.
 Part of the reason that agroforestry practices are so climate friendly (systems without livestock, i.e. ‘normal’ agroforestry like shade grown coffee, for example, are also  estimated by Drawdown to return well on investment, while sequestering 4.45 tons of carbon per hectare per year) is because of what they replace.
  Traditional livestock farming, for instance, is carbon intensive. Trees are cut down for pasture, fossil fuels are used as fertilizer for feed, and that feed is transported across borders, and sometimes the world, using even more fossil fuels.
 Livestock raised in concentrated animal feeding operations (CAFOs), produce more methane than cows that graze on grass. A silvopasture system, on the other hand, involves planting trees in pastures — or at least not cutting them down. Farmers rotate livestock from place to place, allowing soil to hold onto more carbon.
 There are similar benefits to other types of agroforestry practices. Forest farming, for instance, involves growing a variety of crops under a forest canopy — a process that can improve biodiversity and soil quality, and also support the root systems and carbon sequestration potential of farms.
  Etelle Higonnet, senior campaign director at campaign group Mighty Earth, says a growing number of chocolate companies have expressed interest in incorporating agroforestry practices — a marked shift from when she first started advocating for that approach.
 “When we first started talking to chocolate companies and traders about agroforestry, pretty much everybody thought I was a nutter,” she says. “But fast forward three years on and pretty much every major chocolate company and cocoa trader is developing an agroforestry plan.”
 What that means on the ground, though, can vary widely, she says. Most of the time it is a company’s sustainability department that is pushing for agroforestry investment, not the C-suite. Some companies have committed to sourcing 100% of their cacao from agroforestry systems. Others are content with 5% of their cacao coming from farms that use agroforestry.
  What a company considers “agroforestry” can also be squishy, she points out — a situation that makes her and other climate advocates worry about companies using the term to “greenwash,” or essentially pretend to be environmentally friendly without making substantive change.
 “What is agroforestry?” says Simon Konig, executive director of Climate Focus North America. “There is no clear definition. There’s an academic, philosophical definition, but there’s not a practical definition, nothing that says, ‘it includes this many species.’ Basically, agroforestry is anything you want it to be, and anything you want to write on your brochure.”
 He says he has seen cases in South America where people have worked to transform degraded cattle ranches into cocoa plantations. They have planted banana trees alongside cocoa, which needs shade when young. But when the cocoa is five years old and requires more sun, the farmers take out the bananas.
 “They say, ‘it’s agroforestry,’” Konig says. “So there are misunderstandings — there are different objectives and standards.”
 He has been working to produce a practical agroforestry guide for cocoa and chocolate companies. One of the guide’s main takeaways, he says, is that there is not a one-size-fits-all approach to agroforestry. It depends on climate, objectives, markets, and all sorts of other variables.
 This is one of the reasons that agroforestry has been slow to gain investor attention, says LeZaks of the Croatan Institute.
 “There really aren’t the technical resources — the infrastructure, the products — that work to support an agroforestry sector at the moment,” LeZaks says.
  While agroforestry is seen as having significant potential for the carbon offset market, its variability makes it a more complicated agricultural investment. Another challenge to agroforestry investment is time.
 Tree crops take years to produce nuts, berries or timber. This can be a barrier for farmers, who often do not have extra capital to tie up for years.
  “People are bogged down by business as usual,” says Stoian from World Agroforestry. “They have to report to shareholders. Give regular reports. It’s almost contradictory to the long-term nature of agroforestry.”
 This is where Steinberg and Propagate Ventures come in. The first part of the company’s work is to fully analyze a farmer’s operation, Steinberg says. It evaluates business goals, uses geographic information system (GIS) components to map out land, and determines the trees most appropriate for the particular agricultural system. With software analytics, Propagate predicts long-term cost-to-revenue and yields, key information for both farmers and possible private investors.
 After the analysis phase, Propagate helps implement the agroforestry system. It also works to connect third-party investors with farmers, using a revenue-sharing model in which the investor takes a percentage of the profit from harvested tree crops and timber.
 Additionally, Propagate works to arrange commercial contracts with buyers who are interested in adding agroforestry-sourced products to their supply chains.
 “Here’s an opportunity to work with farmers to increase profitability by incorporating tree crops into their operations in a way that’s context specific,” Steinberg says. “And it also starts addressing the ecological challenge that we face in agriculture and beyond.”
 This report is part of Mongabay’s ongoing coverage of trends in global agroforestry, view the full series  here.
 Stephanie Hanes is a journalist and the author of White Man’s Game: Saving Animals, Rebuilding Eden and Other Myths of Conservation in Africa . She teaches at Yale University’s School of the Environment.
title: 投资者表示，农林复合不仅对气候友好，而且有利可图
title_en: Investors say agroforestry isn’t just climate friendly – it’s also profitable

臭名昭著的“漂白剂教会”的四名成员-一名父亲和他的三个成年儿子-在佛罗里达州面临欺诈和藐视法庭的指控，原因是他们将工业漂白剂作为“灵丹妙药”出售。
现年62岁的马克·格勒农和他的儿子们，34岁的乔纳森·格勒农，26岁的乔丹·格勒农和32岁的约瑟夫·格勒农都来自佛罗里达州的布拉登顿，他们于周三被起诉。
迈阿密的联邦检察官声称，这两名男子一直在销售他们的工业漂白产品-名为“奇迹矿物溶液”或彩信-作为治疗新冠肺炎和其他疾病的药物。检察官表示，这两名男子故意违反了4月份发布的一项联邦命令，该命令要求他们停止销售和分销该产品。据称，这家人还向发布命令的联邦法官发出了威胁使用暴力的信件，并警告说，他们可能会举行“韦科舞会”。
根据一份宣誓书，美国食品和药物管理局(Food And Drug Administration)的一名特工一直在秘密购买他们的彩信，并与这些人交换电子邮件。在其中一些电子邮件中，其中一名被告建议这名特工(虚构的)妻子每小时应该喝多少滴漂白剂来治疗她的膀胱癌。
在一名联邦法官命令他们停止销售漂白剂后，据称这些人将他们的在线订单转移到了电子邮件中，并要求客户填写一份调查问卷，询问他们是为政府工作还是为“假新闻”工作。
总而言之，据称这两名男子仅在去年一年就通过出售工业漂白剂作为医疗手段赚取了约50万美元。他们平均每月约为32,000美元。但当他们在今年3月开始兜售这种漂白剂可以治愈新冠肺炎时，他们赚了123,000美元-大约增加了400%的销售额。
这四人是以推广彩信而臭名昭著的创世纪II健康与治疗教会的成员。事实上，根据宣誓书，教会的成立显然是为了规避法律，并将其漂白产品作为圣礼出售。
Genesis是由其他三名被告的父亲马克·格勒农(Mark Grion)和在刑事起诉书中被称为“同谋1号”的吉姆·亨布尔(Jim Humble)于2010年共同创立的。在2020年2月的一次采访中，当马克·格勒农被问及为什么要成立这个教会时，他回答说：“因为你做的一切商业活动都符合通用商法典，好吗？教会与那些法典、法令和法律是完全不同的。这就是为什么牧师可以在教堂公开给孩子喝酒而不会被逮捕的原因。因为这是圣礼…。你不能逮捕我们做我们的圣礼，我知道这一点。所以这就是为什么…。我说我们做个教堂吧。“
宣誓书指出，创世纪自己的网站称它是一座“非宗教教堂”，自称大主教的马克·格勒农(Mark Grion)承认该教堂“与宗教无关”。
尽管如此，教会还是说服顾客相信彩信所谓的治愈能力。该溶液分为两种成分出售：亚氯酸钠溶液和“酸性活化剂”。当它们结合在一起时，会形成二氧化氯，这是一种用于剥离纺织品、纸浆和纸张的工业漂白剂。
Genesis声称彩信可以治疗各种严重的疾病和障碍，包括癌症、阿尔茨海默氏症、自闭症、疟疾、帕金森氏症、多发性硬化症和艾滋病毒/艾滋病-最近还有新冠肺炎，这种疾病是由大流行的新型冠状病毒SARS-CoV-2引起的。
自2010年教会成立以来，联邦监管机构一直在追查它，并警告消费者彩信构成生命威胁。多年来，媒体调查还多次追踪到联合创始人吉姆·汉布尔(Jim Humble)。美国广播公司新闻(ABC News)2016年的一项调查报道称，前内华达州金矿勘探者汉布尔(Humble)已经逃离美国，住在墨西哥瓜达拉哈拉郊外的一个小镇上，美国法律无法触及。亨布尔声称自己是从另一个星系来到地球的。其他报道指出，同样自称大主教的汉布尔仍在美国举办如何销售彩信的研讨会。
2019年8月，FDA向消费者发出了避免MMS的新警告，指出该机构收到了新的报告，称人们在饮用MMS后出现严重呕吐、严重腹泻、脱水和急性肝功能衰竭导致的危及生命的低血压。
时任FDA代理专员内德·夏普莱斯在一份声明中表示：“FDA将继续追踪那些销售这种危险产品的人，并对那些试图逃避FDA规定，向美国公众推销未经批准和潜在危险产品的人采取适当的执法行动。”
今年4月，美国佛罗里达州南区地区法院法官凯瑟琳·威廉姆斯发布临时限制令，要求格勒农和创世纪停止直接或间接分发彩信。5月1日，威廉姆斯法官发布了全面禁制令。
根据卧底FDA特工的调查工作，格勒农夫妇没有遵守命令。然而，这样的秘密工作显然是没有必要的，因为格勒农夫妇直接给威廉姆斯法官写了信和电子邮件，说他们没有遵守。
在4月21日致威廉姆斯法官的一封信中，格勒农夫妇写道：“我们正在对这一不公正的命令进行‘公民抗命’！公民抗命在美国宪法中是允许的，如果可能的话，首先当然是和平的，并补充说，“注意：第二修正案在那里，以防不能和平地进行。”
“创世纪二世健康与疗愈教会不会阻止…。向世界奉献我们的圣礼！信中说，美国司法部和FDA无权管辖我们的教会。
在随后的创世纪播客中，格勒农继续发出威胁，说：“你们得到了第二修正案。对吗？当国会做了不道德的事情，通过了不道德的法律，那就是你拿起枪的时候，对吗？你要韦科吗？他们想要韦科吗？&#34；
他们还指控威廉姆斯法官犯有叛国罪，并说，“你认为我们害怕奥巴马任命的某个违背他们誓言的法官吗？你不是法官。你可能会被除名的，威廉姆斯女士。我们没有遵守[命令]。我不在乎你在做什么。“。
在一份关于这些指控的新闻声明中，佛罗里达州南区联邦检察官阿里安娜·法贾多·奥尔山(Ariana Fajardo Orshan)指出，“这种彩信产品不仅有毒，而且它的分发和使用可能会阻止那些生病的人获得他们所需的合法医疗保健。”她补充说，法院“已经命令被告停止分发本产品；我们不会袖手旁观，因为个人故意违反法院命令，将公众置于危险境地。”
note_en:
Four members of the infamous “Church of Bleach”—a father and his three adult sons—are facing fraud and contempt charges in Florida in connection to selling industrial bleach as a “miracle cure-all.”
 Mark Grenon, 62, and his sons, Jonathan Grenon, 34, Jordan Grenon, 26, and Joseph Grenon, 32, all from Bradenton, Florida, were  charged Wednesday.
 Federal prosecutors in Miami allege that the men have been selling their industrial bleach product—called “Miracle Mineral Solution” or MMS—as a treatment for COVID-19 and other conditions. The prosecutors say the men willfully violated a federal order made in April that they stop selling and distributing the product. The family also allegedly sent letters threatening violence to the federal judge who issued the order and warned that they may stage “a Waco.”
 All along, a special agent with the Food and Drug Administration made undercover purchases of their MMS and exchanged emails with the men,  according to an affidavit. In some of those emails, one of the defendants advised the agent on how many drops of bleach per hour the agent’s (fictional) wife should drink to treat her bladder cancer.
 After a federal judge ordered them to stop selling the bleach, the men allegedly moved their online orders to email and required customers to fill out a survey, asking customers if they worked for the government or “fake news.”
 All in all, the men allegedly made around $500,000 in the last year alone from selling industrial bleach as a medical treatment. They averaged about $32,000 a month. But when they began touting the bleach as a cure for COVID-19 in March of this year, they made $123,000—about a 400 percent increase in sales.
  The four are members of the Genesis II Church of Health and Healing, which is notorious for promoting MMS. In fact, according to the affidavit, the church was formed explicitly to skirt the law and sell its bleach product as sacrament.
 Genesis was co-founded in 2010 by Mark Grenon, the father of the three other defendants, and Jim Humble, who is referred to in the criminal complaint as “Co-Conspirator 1.”  In a February 2020 interview, when Mark Grenon was asked why he formed the church, he responded: “Because everything you do commercially is under the Universal Commercial code, OK? A church is completely separate from that code, statutes, and laws. That’s why a priest can give a kid wine in church publicly and not get arrested. Because it’s a sacrament… You can’t arrest us from doing one of our sacraments, and I knew this. So that’s why… I said let’s do a church.”
 The affidavit notes that Genesis’ own website says it’s a “non-religious church,” and Mark Grenon, who calls himself an Archbishop, has acknowledged that the church “has nothing to do with religion.”
 Still, the church persuades customers to put their faith in the supposed healing powers of MMS. The solution is sold in two components: a solution of sodium chlorite and an “acidic activator.” When combined, they form chlorine dioxide, an industrial bleaching agent used for stripping textiles, pulp, and paper.
 Genesis claims MMS can cure a variety of serious diseases and disorders, including cancer, Alzheimer’s, autism, malaria, Parkinson’s, multiple sclerosis, and HIV/AIDS—and, most recently, COVID-19, the disease caused by the pandemic new coronavirus, SARS-CoV-2.
  Federal regulators have been after the church  since it was formed back in 2010 and have warned consumers that MMS poses life-threatening risks. Media investigations have also tracked down co-founder Jim Humble several times over the years.  A 2016 investigation by ABC News reported that Humble—a former Nevada gold prospector who has claimed to have arrived on Earth from another galaxy—had fled the US and was living in a small town outside Guadalajara, Mexico, out of reach of US law.  Other reports noted that Humble, who also calls himself an Archbishop, still gave seminars in the US on how to sell MMS.
 In August of 2019, the FDA  sent fresh warnings to consumers to avoid MMS, noting that the agency had received new reports of people experiencing severe vomiting, severe diarrhea, life-threatening low blood pressure caused by dehydration and acute liver failure after drinking MMS.
 “The FDA will continue to track those selling this dangerous product and take appropriate enforcement actions against those who attempt to evade FDA regulations and market unapproved and potentially dangerous products to the American public,” then acting FDA commissioner Ned Sharpless said in a statement.
 In April of this year, Judge Kathleen Williams of US District Court for the Southern District of Florida issued a  temporary restraining order requiring the Grenons and Genesis to stop distributing MMS directly or indirectly. On May 1, Judge Williams issued a full injunction order.
 The Grenons did not comply with the order, according the investigative work by the undercover FDA agent. However, such covert work was apparently unnecessary as the Grenons wrote letters and emails directly to Judge Williams saying they were not complying.
 In an April 21 letter to Judge Williams, the Grenons wrote, “We are practicing ‘civil disobedience’ against this unjust order! Civil disobedience is permitted in the US Constitution, peaceably of course at first, if possible," adding, “NOTE: The 2nd Amendment is there in case it can’t be done peaceably.”
 “The Genesis II Church of Health and Healing will not stop… providing our sacraments to the world! The DOJ and FDA have NO authority over our Church," the letter said.
 In subsequent Genesis podcasts, the Grenons continued to make threats, saying, “You’ve got the 2nd [Amendment]. Right? When Congress does immoral things, passes immoral laws, that’s when you pick up guns, right? You want a Waco? Do they want a Waco?"
 They also accused Judge Williams of committing treason and said, “You think we’re afraid of some Obama-appointed judge that broke their oath? You’re no judge. You could be taken out, Ms. Williams. [W]e’re not obeying [the order]. Don’t care what you do.”
 In a press statement on the charges, US Attorney for the Southern District of Florida, Ariana Fajardo Orshan noted that “not only is this MMS product toxic, but its distribution and use may prevent those who are sick from receiving the legitimate healthcare they need.” She added that the court “already has ordered the defendants to stop distributing this product; we will not sit idly by as individuals purposefully violate Court orders and put the public in danger.”
title: 佛罗里达州男子被控出售漂白剂作为新冠肺炎的解药，威胁“韦科”
title_en: Florida men charged with selling bleach as COVID-19 cure, threatening “a Waco”
